name: Old Manali
tagline: The only part of Manali with character left.
why_visit: New Manali is a traffic jam with hotels. Old Manali is the actual reason people came here in the first place — narrow lanes, stone houses, cheap Israeli-backpacker cafes that haven't changed their menus since 2005, and the Manu Temple at the top. It's gentrified compared to a decade ago (rents have tripled, boutique hotels are creeping in), but it still has more soul per square meter than anywhere else in the Kullu Valley. Stay here, not on Mall Road. Walk across the bridge over the Manalsu Nala and you're in a different world from the honking mess below.
highlights: Cafe culture — Drifters, Lazy Dog, Dylan's Toasted & Roasted for coffee and people-watching
Manu Temple — dedicated to the sage Manu, possibly the oldest structure in the area
Walk along the Manalsu Nala stream through apple orchards
Live music at some cafes during peak season (May-June, Sep-Oct)
Street shopping for Kullu shawls, Tibetan jewelry, and hippie clothes
kids_note: Perfectly fine for kids. Flat walking in the village, no altitude issues at 2050m. Some lanes are steep cobblestone — strollers won't work, use a carrier for toddlers. Cafes are relaxed about kids. The stream is shallow enough to paddle in during summer.

name: Solang Valley
tagline: Touristy, loud, and genuinely fun if you stop being a snob about it.
why_visit: Solang gets trashed by travelers who consider themselves above the 'tourist' experience, but honestly — paragliding with the Pir Panjal range as your backdrop is spectacular, and winter skiing here (Jan-Feb) is some of the most accessible in India. Yes, there are too many quad bikes and snow-scooter operators yelling at you. Yes, the gondola queue can be 2 hours. But the ropeway to the top (3300m) gives you genuine high-altitude mountain views, and the paragliding tandem flights are world-class for the price (INR 1500-3000). Go early morning to avoid the Chandigarh-weekend-trip crowds.
highlights: Paragliding tandem flights — 15-minute flights with Himalayan panorama, best Oct-Nov
Solang Gondola (ropeway) to 3300m for snow and views year-round
Skiing and snowboarding in January-February (basic but fun, equipment rental available)
Zorbing and quad biking for the adrenaline-without-skill crowd
Snow point at the top — actual snow even in May
kids_note: Good for kids who like activity. The gondola is safe. Quad bikes are sketchy for small children — avoid. Snow play at the top is the highlight for kids, but dress them warm (it drops to 0°C at 3300m even in summer). Paragliding has a minimum age of ~12 at most operators. The drive from Manali is smooth, 30 minutes.

name: Sethan Village
tagline: Buddhist hamlet with zero tourists and an empty snowfield in winter.
why_visit: Sethan is the kind of place that makes you wonder why anyone stays in Manali. It's a tiny Buddhist village 12km from Manali (30-minute drive up a winding road), with maybe 20 houses, a small monastery, and views that are better than anything you'll get from Mall Road. In winter (Dec-Feb), the entire meadow above the village turns into an untouched snowfield — no queues, no snow-scooter operators, just you and the mountains. A handful of homestays have opened in the last few years, but it's still genuinely off the beaten path. The drive up is half the experience — hairpin bends through dense deodar forest.
highlights: Winter snowfield — completely empty, bring your own snow shoes or just wade through
Igloo stays available Dec-Feb (a few operators run igloo camping, book ahead)
Small Buddhist gompa with prayer flags against a mountain backdrop
Starting point for Hampta Pass trek (the classic Manali multi-day)
Apple orchards and meadows in summer — quiet picnic territory
kids_note: Fine for kids 5+. The road up is steep and narrow — can make carsick kids uncomfortable. The village itself is flat and safe. No medical facilities — carry a first aid kit. Winter snow play is excellent for kids but temperatures drop to -10°C at night, so come prepared. No restaurants — homestays serve meals.

name: Vashisht
tagline: Hot springs, a stone temple, and none of the Mall Road headache.
why_visit: Vashisht sits on the hillside across the river from Manali, close enough to walk (2km from Old Manali) but far enough to feel separate. The main draw is the Vashisht Temple — an ancient stone and wood structure with natural hot sulphur springs in the temple complex. The springs have separate bathing pools for men and women (free, maintained by the temple trust). The water is genuinely hot and supposedly therapeutic. The village has a few guesthouses and cafes that are cheaper than Old Manali and quieter than everything else. It's a good base if Old Manali feels too buzzy for you.
highlights: Vashisht Temple hot springs — free, natural sulphur water, separate pools for men and women
Ancient stone-and-wood temple architecture (thought to be 4000 years old, grain of salt needed)
Walk from Vashisht to Old Manali via the forest trail (45 min, lovely)
Cheaper guesthouses than Old Manali with similar views
Turkish and German bakeries — legacy of the 90s hippie trail
kids_note: Good for kids. The hot springs are safe — water is warm, not scalding. The pools can be crowded mid-day, go early morning with kids. The walk to Old Manali is flat and safe. Village lanes are steep in places but manageable. Same altitude as Manali, no concerns.

name: Naggar
tagline: Skip Manali, stay here. Castle, art gallery, apple orchards, actual peace.
why_visit: Naggar was the capital of the Kullu kingdom for 1400 years before they moved to Kullu town. It should be the main destination in this valley, not Manali. Naggar Castle (now an HPTDC hotel — you can stay in a 500-year-old castle for INR 2000) sits on a ridge with 270-degree valley views. The Nicholas Roerich Art Gallery is in the Russian painter's former estate, surrounded by gardens with Himalayan backdrops. The village is small, walkable, quiet, and surrounded by apple orchards. Coming here from Manali takes 45 minutes but feels like leaving the circus for the countryside. Seriously, if you have 3 nights in the Kullu Valley, spend 2 here.
highlights: Naggar Castle — 500-year-old castle converted to heritage hotel, open to visitors
Nicholas Roerich Art Gallery — haunting Himalayan paintings in the artist's estate
Urusvati Himalayan Folk Art Museum next to Roerich Gallery
Jana waterfall — 3km walk from Naggar, uncrowded
Apple orchards everywhere — visit during harvest (August-October) to buy direct from farmers
kids_note: Excellent for kids. Lower altitude than Manali (1760m), no issues. The castle is fascinating for children. The grounds around Roerich Gallery are big enough for kids to run around. Jana waterfall walk is easy and flat. The village is car-free in the center. This is arguably the best family base in the Kullu Valley.

name: Atal Tunnel (North Exit — Lahaul)
tagline: Drive through Asia's longest highway tunnel and emerge into Lahaul. Sissu waterfall on the other side.
why_visit: The Atal Tunnel (Rohtang Tunnel) is 9.02km long, cuts through the Pir Panjal range at 3000m, and opened in 2020. Before it existed, getting from Manali to Lahaul/Spiti required crossing Rohtang Pass (open only June-October, weather permitting). Now you drive through in 15 minutes and emerge into a completely different landscape — arid, Tibetan-looking, stark. The north portal exit at Sissu is worth a stop — Sissu waterfall is visible from the road, and the Tandi confluence (Chandra and Bhaga rivers meeting) is 20 minutes ahead. The tunnel itself is a genuine engineering marvel, and the temperature drop on the other side is dramatic.
highlights: 9.02km tunnel — longest highway tunnel above 3000m, drive-through in 15 minutes
Sissu waterfall visible immediately after the north portal exit
Tandi confluence — Chandra and Bhaga rivers meet, 20km from tunnel exit
Landscape transforms from green Kullu to arid Lahaul in one tunnel length
Open year-round (unlike Rohtang Pass), making winter Lahaul trips possible
kids_note: The tunnel drive is fine for kids — well-lit, ventilated, smooth road. The north side is at 3060m so mild altitude caution for young children if you spend extended time. Sissu is a quick roadside stop, no effort needed. The real consideration is whether you're continuing to Lahaul/Spiti (long drive, limited facilities) or just doing a return trip through the tunnel.
time_needed: 2-3 hours (return trip from Manali including stops)

name: Bhagsu (Bhagsunag)
tagline: Waterfall, Shiva Cafe at the top, backpacker prices. Touristy but iconic.
why_visit: Bhagsu is the walk every McLeodGanj visitor does on their first day. The Bhagsunag Temple (ancient Shiva temple with a spring-fed pool) is at the bottom, and a 20-minute uphill walk takes you past the Bhagsu waterfall to Shiva Cafe at the top — an open-air cafe with cushions, chai, and the entire Kangra Valley spread below you. Yes, it's touristy. Yes, every travel blogger has the same photo. But it's touristy because it genuinely delivers. The waterfall is best after monsoon (Sep-Oct) when it's actually powerful. The walk is easy enough for anyone reasonably fit. Avoid weekends when Chandigarh and Delhi day-trippers overrun the trail.
highlights: Shiva Cafe — open-air hilltop cafe with Kangra Valley panorama, the iconic McLeod experience
Bhagsunag waterfall — best after monsoon, modest rest of the year
Bhagsunag Temple — ancient Shiva temple at the base, natural spring pool
Budget accommodation — guesthouses here are 30-50% cheaper than McLeod main market
Street food row along the main Bhagsu road — momos, thukpa, falafel wraps
kids_note: Walk to Shiva Cafe is uphill and rocky in the last section — manageable for kids 6+ but not stroller-friendly. The waterfall area has slippery rocks, hold young children's hands. Bhagsunag Temple pool is shallow and safe. The main Bhagsu road is flat and easy. Overall fine for families, just skip the last steep section if kids are under 5.

name: Dharamkot
tagline: Above McLeod, quieter, yoga central. The foreigner long-stay village.
why_visit: Dharamkot is where the people who came to McLeodGanj for a week and stayed for three months end up living. It sits above McLeodGanj (15-minute steep uphill walk), and has become the yoga-and-meditation village — Tushita Meditation Centre runs 10-day silent retreats here, there are half a dozen yoga shalas, and the cafes serve the kind of food that uses words like 'activated' and 'probiotic' unironically. The vibe is quieter and more intentional than McLeod or Bhagsu. If you want to actually practice yoga, meditate, or just wake up to Dhauladhar views without the honking, stay here instead of McLeod proper. Gallu Devi Temple at the top of the village is the start of the Triund trek.
highlights: Tushita Meditation Centre — 10-day Introduction to Buddhism retreats (book months ahead)
Multiple yoga shalas — Himalayan Iyengar Yoga Centre, Dharamkot Yoga, others
Gallu Devi Temple — starting point for the Triund trek
Trek Lazy cafe and Morgan's Place for foreigner-friendly food
Dhauladhar range views from the village edge — better than McLeod because you're higher
kids_note: The uphill walk from McLeod is steep but short. The village itself is small and safe — cars can barely get through. No specific kid activities, but the open spaces and forest trails are good for children who like outdoors. Not ideal if you need stroller access or lots of kid amenities. The altitude (1880m) is fine.
time_needed: half day to multi-day (depending on retreat/yoga)

name: Naddi
tagline: Sunset viewpoint above McLeod. Dhauladhar panorama. Most tourists miss this.
why_visit: Naddi is a 10-minute drive above McLeodGanj and somehow 90% of visitors never make it here. The Naddi viewpoint gives you an unobstructed 180-degree panorama of the Dhauladhar range — it's genuinely one of the best mountain views you'll get in Himachal without trekking. Come for sunset when the peaks turn gold-to-pink. The village has a few hotels with mountain-facing rooms that cost half of what McLeod charges for a wall view. There's also a small lake (Dal Lake, not the Kashmir one) nearby that's pleasant for a 30-minute walk but not worth a special trip. The real draw is the viewpoint and the silence.
highlights: Naddi viewpoint — 180-degree Dhauladhar panorama, best at sunset
Mountain-facing hotels at 50% of McLeod prices
Dal Lake — small, peaceful, 15-minute walk from the viewpoint
Fewer tourists than anywhere else in the McLeod orbit
Clear night skies — noticeably less light pollution than McLeod
kids_note: Very easy for kids. Drive up, walk 2 minutes to viewpoint, enjoy. No steep trails, no altitude issues. Dal Lake walk is flat and safe. The only downside is fewer restaurants — you'll eat at your hotel or drive to McLeod. Perfect sunset stop with kids before heading back down.

name: Triund Basecamp & Trek
tagline: The classic day trek. 9km return. Dhauladhar wall at your feet. Now ticketed and regulated.
why_visit: Triund is probably the most popular day trek in all of Himachal, and for good reason — it's accessible (starts from Dharamkot, no technical difficulty), short enough to do in a day (4.5km one way, 3-4 hours up), and the payoff is absurd. You emerge from the tree line onto a grassy ridge with the entire Dhauladhar range directly in front of you, so close it feels like a wall. On the other side, the Kangra Valley drops away into haze. The trek is now regulated — you need a ticket from the forest department, and overnight camping is limited to reduce the garbage problem that nearly ruined this place. Go on a weekday if you can. The last 1km (the '22 curves' section) is steep enough to make unfit trekkers question their life choices.
highlights: Dhauladhar range at arm's length — the summit view is worth every step of the climb
Manageable for beginners — no technical sections, well-marked trail
The '22 curves' final section — steep zigzag that sorts the trekkers from the tourists
Camping at the top (limited permits, book in advance through forest department)
Magic View Cafe and other trail cafes for chai and Maggi refueling stops
kids_note: Not recommended for children under 10. The trek is 9km return with 1000m elevation gain — that's a serious hike for small legs. The last km is steep and exposed. Kids 10-14 who are active and used to walking can manage, but start early and go slow. No railing or safety infrastructure. Altitude at the top (2828m) is fine physiologically but combined with the exertion it can exhaust younger children. Carry enough water — trail cafes sometimes run dry.
time_needed: full day (6-8 hours return including rest)

name: Gushaini
tagline: Main base for Tirthan. Riverside homestays. Trout fishing. GHNP gateway.
why_visit: Gushaini is where most Tirthan Valley stays happen, and it's earned that spot. The village sits on the Tirthan River — proper riverside, as in your homestay balcony might overhang the water. The river has brown and rainbow trout (catch-and-release fishing with license, about INR 500/day from the forest department), and the sound of it becomes your ambient soundtrack. This is the main entry point for the Great Himalayan National Park — the GHNP buffer zone starts a few km upstream. Homestays here range from basic (INR 800/night) to boutique (INR 3000-5000), all family-run, all serving home-cooked Himachali food. There's no mall road, no traffic, no tourist infrastructure in the annoying sense. Just the river, the forest, and families who've been here for generations.
highlights: Riverside homestays — Raju's, Tirthan Anglers Retreat, River Retreat among many good options
Trout fishing in the Tirthan River (license required, catch-and-release)
GHNP buffer zone entry — guided nature walks start from here
Swimming holes in the river — locals will point you to the safe spots
Home-cooked Himachali food — trout, siddu, rajma-chawal, fresh from the kitchen
kids_note: Excellent for kids. The river is the main attraction — shallow sections are safe for wading but always supervise (the current picks up after rain). Homestay families are typically welcoming to children. Low altitude (1550m), no concerns. No harsh terrain. The biggest risk is the narrow mountain road getting here from Aut — can be queasy for carsick kids. Essentially a countryside vacation with a river, ideal for families.

name: Jibhi
tagline: Pretty riverside village emerging as its own destination. Waterfalls, cafes, Instagram bait.
why_visit: Jibhi has blown up on Instagram in the last 3-4 years, and it's a double-edged sword. The village is genuinely beautiful — a small river runs through it, there are waterfalls nearby (Jibhi waterfall is a 15-minute walk), the forest is thick and green, and the wooden architecture is traditional Himachali. But the influencer wave has brought boutique cafes, treehouse stays, and weekend crowds from Delhi that the village wasn't designed for. Visit on a weekday and it's still lovely. Visit on a long weekend and you'll wonder if you accidentally drove to Shimla Mall Road. The saving grace is that walking 15 minutes in any direction from the main road takes you into genuine quiet. Come here for the waterfalls, the short hikes, and the cafes — but if you want real solitude, base at Gushaini instead.
highlights: Jibhi waterfall — 15-minute walk from the village, best after monsoon
Treehouse and cottage stays — multiple options, book ahead on weekends
Shringa Rishi Temple — ancient stone temple on a hill above the village
Chehni Kothi — massive traditional Himachali tower, 45-minute hike from Jibhi
Cafes and bakeries — good coffee has arrived in the valley, for better or worse
kids_note: Great for kids. Flat walking to the waterfall, treehouse stays are exciting for children, the river is gentle here. Low altitude, no concerns. The Chehni Kothi hike is steep and not suitable for kids under 8. Main risk is the same narrow mountain roads getting here. More food options than Gushaini if your kids are fussy eaters.

name: Shoja
tagline: Near-empty, gorgeous views, ancient tower architecture. The quiet one.
why_visit: Shoja is what Jibhi was five years ago — a tiny village on the road between Banjar and Jalori Pass, with almost nothing there except some of the best views in the valley and a handful of guesthouses. The Serolsar Lake trail starts from nearby Jalori Pass, and the village is close enough to be a base for that hike. But Shoja's real asset is its obscurity. The few tourists who pass through are on their way somewhere else. Stay a night. Walk through the village to see traditional Himachali tower houses (kathkuni architecture — stone and wood, no nails). Watch the sunset over the Tirthan Valley from above. You'll have it to yourself.
highlights: Traditional kathkuni tower architecture — stone and wood construction, centuries old
Valley views from the ridge — Tirthan and Banjar valleys visible below
Proximity to Jalori Pass (10km) — base for Serolsar Lake and Raghupur Fort hikes
Waterfall Point — 2km walk from the village, small but beautiful
Almost zero crowds even in peak season
kids_note: Fine for kids but there's nothing specifically for them to do — it's a quiet mountain village. The walks are easy. Slightly higher altitude (2200m) but no issue for healthy children. Very limited food options — you eat what the guesthouse serves. Good for families who enjoy being outdoors and don't need structured activities.

name: Jalori Pass
tagline: 3120m drive-up pass. Serolsar Lake is a flat 2km walk from the top. Raghupur Fort for the ambitious.
why_visit: Jalori Pass is one of the lowest motorable passes in Himachal, which means you can drive to 3120m without any of the Rohtang/Kunzum drama. The pass itself is a wide clearing with a small temple, prayer flags, and dhabas serving hot chai and Maggi. The reason to come here is the 5km roundtrip walk to Serolsar Lake — a sacred emerald-green lake surrounded by thick forest, with a small wooden temple dedicated to Budhi Nagin on its shore. The walk is flat to gentle uphill through beautiful oak and conifer forest. For more effort, the Raghupur Fort hike (3300m) goes in the other direction — steeper, 6km return, but the fort ruins and the 360-degree views from the top are worth it.
highlights: Serolsar Lake — emerald-green sacred lake, 2.5km gentle walk from the pass
Raghupur Fort — ruined fort at 3300m, 360-degree panorama, 3km steep hike from pass
Drive-up accessibility — one of the easiest 3000m+ passes to reach in Himachal
Chai and Maggi at the pass dhabas — the mountain cliche that never disappoints
Forest walk to the lake through dense oak and conifer — genuinely beautiful any season
kids_note: Serolsar Lake walk is doable for kids 5+ — gentle terrain, well-marked path, 2.5km each way. Raghupur Fort hike is NOT suitable for kids under 12 (steep, exposed, 3300m altitude). At the pass itself, the altitude (3120m) deserves attention — don't let kids run around wildly on arrival, give them 30 minutes to adjust. Warm clothing essential, it's significantly colder than the valley below even in summer.

name: Great Himalayan National Park (Buffer Zone Entry)
tagline: UNESCO World Heritage. Guided treks mandatory. The real wild Himachal.
why_visit: GHNP is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the most biodiverse protected areas in the western Himalayas — snow leopards, Himalayan brown bears, musk deer, 200+ bird species, and temperate forests that haven't been logged. You cannot enter without a guide and permit (arranged through the park office in Sai Ropa or through your homestay). The buffer zone treks (1-2 days) give you thick forest, river crossings, and a genuine sense of wilderness without extreme difficulty. The deeper treks (Tirthan headwaters, 3-5 days) are proper backcountry expeditions. The park is doing conservation right — local communities earn from guiding and homestays, poaching is down, and the wildlife is recovering. This is not a zoo-safari park; it's actual wilderness with real animals you'll probably hear but may not see.
highlights: UNESCO World Heritage Site — one of the most biodiverse temperate zones in the Himalayas
Guided nature walks in the buffer zone (2-6 hours, arranged through Sai Ropa visitor centre)
Multi-day treks into the core zone — Tirthan headwaters (3-5 days) for serious trekkers
Birdwatching — 200+ species including western tragopan and Himalayan monal
Community-based ecotourism — guides and porters are all local, revenue stays in the valley
kids_note: Buffer zone walks (2-3 hours) are suitable for kids 8+. The terrain is uneven forest trail — no strollers, good shoes needed. Multi-day core zone treks are NOT for children. The buffer zone has no altitude issues (1700-2200m). Kids who like nature will love the birds, butterflies, and river. Carry snacks and water — no shops inside the park. Guide mandatory, which is actually great for kids as guides point out wildlife.
time_needed: half day (buffer zone) to 3-5 days (core zone trek)

name: Sangla
tagline: Baspa Valley's main town. Kamru Fort above. More facilities than Chitkul.
why_visit: Sangla is the practical base for exploring the Baspa Valley. It has what Chitkul doesn't — ATMs (unreliable but present), a few restaurants, proper hotels alongside homestays, and phone signal that mostly works. The town sits in a wide section of the valley with the Baspa River below and apple orchards on every slope. Kamru Fort perches on the hill above town — a steep 20-minute climb to a wooden fort that's been there for 500+ years. The town itself is functional rather than charming, but the valley setting is spectacular. If Chitkul accommodation is full (common in peak season), Sangla is your fallback and it's only 20km downvalley. The morning drive from Sangla to Chitkul along the Baspa is one of the prettiest 45-minute drives in India.
highlights: Kamru Fort — ancient wooden fort on the hill above town, 20-minute steep walk
Baspa River running through town — the sound and the views are constant
Apple and cherry orchards on every slope — visit during harvest (Aug-Oct) for fresh fruit
Bering Nag Temple — traditional wooden temple in the village center
Morning drive to Chitkul along the Baspa — 20km of some of India's best valley scenery
kids_note: Good for kids. The town is walkable and the altitude (2680m) is manageable for healthy children — give them a day to acclimatize if coming from the plains. Kamru Fort climb is steep and the final section has narrow wooden stairs, hold young children's hands. The river is fast-moving here, keep kids away from the banks. More food options than Chitkul if your kids are picky eaters.

name: Rakcham
tagline: Between Sangla and Chitkul. Equally beautiful, quieter. Often overlooked.
why_visit: Rakcham is the village everyone drives through on the way to Chitkul and barely notices. This is a mistake. It sits at 2900m on the Baspa River, has maybe 50 houses with traditional slate-roof architecture, a small ITBP checkpost, and absolutely none of the weekend-tourist energy that Chitkul now has. A few homestays have opened in recent years, and the views upvalley toward Chitkul are identical to what people drive another 10km to see. The village has a functioning campsite by the river. Walk 10 minutes in any direction and you're alone with the mountains. If Chitkul's fame has made it too crowded for your taste, Rakcham delivers the same experience with one-tenth the people.
highlights: Traditional slate-roof village — photogenic and authentic
River camping — campsite by the Baspa with mountain views on all sides
Almost no tourists — even in peak season, you'll be one of a handful of visitors
Identical Baspa Valley scenery to Chitkul, 10km closer
Short walks to viewpoints in any direction from the village
kids_note: Fine for kids 5+. The altitude (2900m) needs attention — allow a day to acclimatize, especially if coming directly from the plains. The village is small and safe but facilities are minimal — no pharmacy, no doctor. The river is fast and cold, keep kids well back from the banks. Homestay food is simple but filling. Good for families who are comfortable with basic infrastructure.

name: Kamru Fort
tagline: Ancient wooden fort above Sangla. Claimed 500+ years old. Inner sanctum requires permission.
why_visit: Kamru Fort is the most interesting single structure in the Baspa Valley. It sits on a rocky outcrop above Sangla, and the climb to it (20 minutes, steep stone steps) takes you through the village and up to a complex that includes a tower fort, a wooden temple to Kamaksha Devi, and a courtyard with carved wooden balconies. The architecture is kathkuni — alternating stone and wood without nails, the same earthquake-resistant technique used across Kinnaur for centuries. The inner sanctum of the temple requires permission from the local deity's priest, and foreigners are typically not allowed inside (though you can ask). The views from the fort terrace — Baspa Valley in both directions, snow peaks above — justify the climb even if you can't enter the sanctum.
highlights: Kathkuni architecture — stone and wood construction, no nails, centuries old
Kamaksha Devi Temple within the fort complex — active place of worship
Terrace views of the entire Baspa Valley and surrounding snow peaks
Wooden carvings on balconies and doorways — some of the finest in Kinnaur
The climb through Sangla village to the fort — see daily life in a Kinnauri town
kids_note: The climb is steep with stone steps — manageable for kids 6+ but tiring for smaller children. No railings on some sections. The fort courtyard is small, not much space for kids to run around. The wooden temple interior has low doorways and dark passages — hold children's hands. Total visit including climb is about 90 minutes. The altitude (2800m) is not an issue if you've already acclimatized in Sangla.
